title: Sourdough discard waffles
subtitle: Crisp, tangy waffles made with sourdough discard
excerpt: Crisp, golden waffles that use sourdough starter discard for a subtle tang and an open, airy crumb. The discard replaces some of the flour and liquid in a standard batter, and a small amount of baking powder ensures reliable lift regardless of the starter's activity level. The result is noticeably better than a plain waffle: more flavour and a more complex texture from the fermented batter.
sourceNotes: Sourdough discard waffles are a contemporary adaptation of the American waffle tradition, documented in home-baking literature from at least the early twentieth century. Marion Harland, Common Sense in the Household (1871, freely available at Project Gutenberg), includes yeast-raised waffle recipes as a standard American breakfast item. The use of sourdough discard as a flavouring and partial leavener in waffles is an extension of this tradition, widely described in home-baking guides from the 1920s onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely on a rack then freeze in a single layer. Reheat in a toaster or hot oven for 5 minutes to restore crispness.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the batter for 16 waffles. Cook in sequence; keep finished waffles in a 120°C oven directly on the rack so steam does not soften the crust.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can be mixed the night before and refrigerated. Bring to room temperature and give it a gentle stir before cooking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Cooked in a waffle iron. Preheat the iron fully before adding batter; a properly hot iron is the main driver of crispness.

glossaryTerms.definition: The portion of sourdough starter removed and discarded before feeding, usually 100 to 200 g. At the point of discard, the starter is past its peak activity and not strong enough to leaven a loaf, but it retains its acidity and flavour. In waffle batter, it contributes tang and a more open texture without needing to be active. Any hydration discard works; a 100% hydration (equal weights flour and water) discard is the most common. Do not use discard that smells off, pink, or strongly of acetone.

content.text: These waffles depend on a hot waffle iron, not on the starter's activity. The

is there for flavour, not lift; baking powder does the leavening. Preheat the iron until it is fully hot before adding the first ladleful, and do not open it early: the waffle releases naturally when it is done. Forced early opening tears the surface.

Preheat the waffle iron. Whisk together the sourdough discard, milk, eggs, melted butter, and vanilla extract in a large bowl until smooth. Add the flour, baking powder, sugar, and salt and whisk briefly until just combined. Do not overmix; a few small lumps are fine. The batter should be pourable but not watery.

Lightly oil or butter the waffle iron if it is not non-stick. Pour in enough batter to just fill the grid, close, and cook for 3 to 5 minutes until golden and crisp, and the iron stops steaming. Do not open early. Transfer to a rack or a low oven while you cook the remaining waffles. Serve with butter and maple syrup, or with fresh fruit and cream.
